18.1.label: Question 18.1
18.1.synopsis: (i) Large proportion in single asset: manager believes asset under-priced/will perform well, or investment medium only available in large units (eg central London office blocks). (ii) Undesirable to invest in small number: creates too much risk that asset performs badly; inadmissible assets; liquidity problem. (iii) Acceptable concentration: zero probability of default, assets appropriate for liabilities in nature and term, assets admissible; eg conventional gilts for fixed monetary liabilities provided term appropriate
18.1.question: N/A

(i).synopsis: Asset under-priced or expected to perform particularly well; investment medium only available in large units eg central London office blocks
(i).question: Outline why an investment manager might want to put a relatively large proportion of the funds in a single asset.

Holding a large proportion of assets in: An investment manager may want to put a large proportion of assets in a single asset if they think that the asset will perform particularly well (ie it is under-priced).
They might also be attracted to an investment medium that only comes in large units (eg central London office blocks).

(ii).synopsis: Creates too much risk that asset performs badly; inadmissible assets; liquidity problem
(ii).question: Give reasons why it may be undesirable for a company to invest solely in a small number of assets.

Reasons undesirable to invest in small n: It would be undesirable to invest in one or a few assets because this will generally create too much risk (ie risk that the asset performs badly).
It would also be undesirable if part of the assets were then inadmissible.
There might also be a liquidity problem.

(iii).synopsis: Acceptable if zero probability of default, assets appropriate for liabilities in nature and term, and admissible; conventional gilts fit for fixed monetary liabilities provided term is appropriate
(iii).question: Suggest when a concentration of assets may be acceptable as part of an asset portfolio.

When concentration of assets would be ac: Concentration of assets would be acceptable if:
• there is zero probability of default,
• the assets are appropriate for the liabilities in nature and term, and
Conventional gilts fit this requirement for fixed monetary liabilities, provided the term is appropriate.

18.2.label: Question 18.2
18.2.scenario: An actuary is responsible for choosing the assets of a typical health insurance company, with a moderate level of solvency. They are allowed to select just one asset category from fixed-interest bonds, equities, property, index-linked bonds and cash.

(i).synopsis: Cash is probably best: no marketability or asset value problems, secure in monetary terms, some limited inflation protection via link between short-term interest rates and inflation; a portfolio of short-term index-linked bonds might also be acceptable
(i).question: Suggest the best asset category to choose, giving reasons for your answer.

Best asset category: Cash might be probably the best single asset category because:
• there are no problems with marketability or asset values
• it is secure in monetary terms
• there is a link between short-term interest rates and inflation, so some limited inflation protection.
A portfolio of short-term index-linked bonds might also be acceptable.

(ii).synopsis: (a) Very low free assets: cash is the only acceptable answer. (b) Massive free assets: fund manager could put the whole lot into equities (and hope there is not a repeat of the 1973/1974 UK crash when equities fell by 73%)
(ii).question:
Explain how the answer to part (i) would change if the company had:

(a) very low free assets, eg assets = liabilities + MCR

(b) massive free assets, eg assets = 3 × (liabilities + MCR).

(a) Very low free assets: For a company with very low free assets, cash is the only acceptable answer.
(b) Massive free assets: For a company with very large free assets then the fund manager could put the whole lot into equities (and hope that there's not a repeat of the 1973 / 1974 UK crash when equities fell by 73%).

(iii).synopsis: Not suitable for PMI: equity values may fall dramatically in short term causing shortfall to meet claims or even insolvency; not as liquid or marketable as cash which is usually needed short-term for these liabilities; large sales of equities needed to pay claims may depress market price causing shortfall; admissibility problems
(iii).question: Explain why equities may not be the most suitable asset category to match PMI liabilities.

Why equities may not be suitable to matc: Equities are not most suitable for PMI as:
• equity values may fall dramatically in the short term causing a shortfall to meet claims, or even insolvency
• equities are not as liquid or marketable as cash, and cash is usually needed in the short term for these liabilities
• large sales of equities needed to pay claims may depress the market price causing a shortfall

(iv).synopsis: Equities may form significant proportion because: large free assets seeking higher expected returns; majority of liabilities may be long-term business (eg pre-funded LTCI) best matched by equities providing inflation protection; proprietary companies may prefer franked income; company may have predominantly unit-linked long-term contracts with unit funds invested in equities with little risk to the company
(iv).question: In contrast, explain why equities may form a significant proportion of a health insurer's investment portfolio.

Why equities may form a significant prop: Equities may form a significant proportion of a health insurer's portfolio because:
• the company may have large free assets and be seeking higher expected returns
• the majority of liabilities may be in respect of long-term business (eg pre-funded LTCI) and be best matched by equities providing inflation protection
• proprietary companies may have a preference for franked income
• the company may have predominantly unit-linked long-term contracts, with unit funds invested in equities with little risk to the company.

18.3.label: Question 18.3
18.3.scenario:
A new, small, proprietary health and care insurance company is planning to sell a wide variety of both long-term and short-term health and care insurance products. It is planning to grow market share early on by using innovative marketing techniques.

One of the directors has suggested that as claims will not be paid for some time yet, the total assets should be invested in equities in order to maximise long-term returns.
18.3.question: N/A

(i).question: Discuss the points that could be made in a response to this suggestion.

Response to the suggestion: The director is correct to say that the aim should be to maximise returns. [½]
They are also correct in implying that equities traditionally provide returns that have in the long-term been higher than other assets such as bonds. [½]
However, there are many other considerations that should be borne in mind.

For the majority of the classes of health insurance business, investment returns are not very important, as the majority of the products are protection or short-term products, and so have low reserves. [½]
The effect of maximising returns may therefore not be significant. [½]
As the company is new and small, access to capital is limited. [½]
This is particularly important since rapid expansion is envisaged, which will put a strain on capital. [½]
Equities can provide a good protection against long-term inflation, and may be a reasonable match to the long-term health insurance classes, if benefits are inflation-linked. [1]
They are also very marketable. [½]
However, market values can be very volatile. [½]
For a small company in its infancy, and growing, a fall in values could be disastrous. [½]
It is also unlikely that the company would have the expertise to deal with such portfolios in-house. [½]
Equities would be totally unsuitable for the short-term business, such as PMI. [½]
For short-term health business, claims do not generally take a long time to appear and be settled. [½]

The company's solvency capital will be crucial in the early years. For short-term business, equities will provide a poor match for the solvency capital, which should be matched by short-term investments such as cash and short fixed-interest investments. [1]
For long-term business, the solvency capital is best invested similarly to that backing the technical reserves, which depends on the class of business. Equities may be suitable for some of these. [1]
In any case, having the entire assets in equities gives no diversification by asset class. [½]

As the company grows, and providing business turns out to be profitable, it may be possible to move slowly towards a long-term position with greater holdings in equities. However, this would probably not be for some time. [1]
The situation should be reviewed regularly and an appropriate investment strategy put in place following each review. [½]
It is also important to give shareholders as stable a profit stream as possible, even in the early years of the company's life – this can prove difficult using equities. [½]
[Maximum 8]
